title: Ensure cross-origin resource sharing does not allow all  origins

input_row.title: Ensure cross-origin resource sharing does not allow all  origins
input_row.description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how applications interact with resources hosted on different domains.
input_row.rationale: Restrict CORS to only trusted origins to help enforce proper access control and reduce exposure to malicious cross-origin requests.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Service. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under API, click CORS. 6. Ensure Allowed Origins does not include *. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list apps: az webapp list For each app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az webapp deployment slot list --resource-group --name For each deployment slot, run the following command to get the CORS allowed origins setting: az resource show --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --query properties.cors.allowedOrigins Ensure that the response does not include *. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: cae7c12e-764b-4c87-841a-fdc6675d196f - Name: 'App Service app slots should not have CORS configured to allow every resource to access your apps'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Service. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under API, click CORS. 6. Under Allowed Origins, delete the entry that equals *. 7. Specify the origins that should be allowed to make cross-origin calls. 8. Click Save. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command to update the allowed origins array to contain only the origins that should be allowed: az resource update --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --set properties.cors.allowedOrigins="['']"
input_row.impact: Configuration is required to ensure that the appropriate origins have access. Setting up a proper CORS policy can be fairly complex and an incorrect setting could permit Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). The "caveat" is that if the app being deployed is a PUBLIC API, a wildcard "*" CORS policy is absolutely necessary.
input_row.default_value: By default, CORS is not configured.

step1_initial_assessment:
1. **Analysis of Control Data**:
   - **ID**: 2.2.17
   - **Title/Description/Rationale**: The control pertains to ensuring Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) does not allow all origins in Azure App Services. This reduces exploitation risks from malicious cross-origin requests.
   - **Audit**: Specifies detailed steps to audit using Azure Portal and CLI, which is crafted for Azure’s environment.
   - **Remediation**: Also outlines steps to remediate the CORS configurations in Azure.
   - **Impact**: Highlights potential risks if CORS is configured incorrectly.
   - **References**: Provides URLs for further reading.

2. **Audit Assessment**: 
   - The audit method provided is specifically tailored to Azure services and does not directly translate to AWS services, as AWS has different mechanisms for handling CORS (especially with services like S3 and API Gateway).

3. **AWS Architecture Considerations**:
   - In AWS, similar CORS settings apply particularly in S3 buckets and API Gateway. CORS can be set for S3 using the `put_bucket_cors` method in Boto3 and retrieved with `get_bucket_cors`, while API Gateway has its own CORS configuration.

4. **Feasibility of Automated Checks**:
   - The control primarily focuses on configuration that can be audited and remediated, which AWS services do support through APIs. AWS APIs exist that allow for the assessment of CORS settings on certain services.

5. **Auditable with AWS APIs**:
   - The property being checked (CORS settings on buckets in S3 or APIs in API Gateway) is available through API calls — which can be automated via Boto3 or AWS CLI.

**AUDIT APPROACH**: Automated

**PROGRAM NAME**: aws_s3_bucket_cors_configuration_allowed_origins_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS**:
- **AWS API Calls/Boto3 Methods to Use**:
  - Use `boto3.client('s3').get_bucket_cors(Bucket='bucket-name')` to retrieve CORS configuration for S3 buckets.
  - Check configuration against allowed origins to ensure there isn’t an entry for `*` (wildcard).
  
- **AWS CLI Commands**:
  - Use `aws s3api get-bucket-cors --bucket bucket-name` to list the CORS configuration on specified S3 buckets.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate**:
  - S3 buckets within the AWS account and possibly API Gateway APIs if applicable.

- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate**:
  - For S3 CORS configuration, validate if the allowed origins does not include `*` and contains valid specified origins instead.

- **Pass/Fail Criteria**:
  - The control passes if the `AllowedOrigins` array does not contain `*` (wildcard). A failure occurs if there is a forgone entry indicating that all origins are permitted.

This approach provides an automated means of ensuring compliance regarding CORS configurations in relevant AWS services, aligned with the principles laid out in the reference Azure control.
